details.text: Derivative: سَارِبٌ (Saarib) comes from سَرِبَ – يَسْرَبُ – سَرَبًا which can mean “to flow” like water flowing or “to go about” on one’s way. سَارِبٌ (Saarib) is used to refer to someone who is conspicuous and out in the open.

Derivative: مُعَقِّبَاتٌ comes from عَقَّبَ – يُعّقِّبُ – تَعْقِيبًا which means “to follow” and “succeed” whilst مُعَقِّب (Mu’aqqib) is its اِسْم الفَاعِل (Ism ul-Faa’il – Active participle) form. مُعَقِّبَاتٌ (Mu’aqqibaat) are those who “follow closely or pursue in succession” and refers to the Angels that follow and pursue each person continuously where one group of them follows after another.

Derivative: وَالٍ (Waalin) is the الفَاعِل (Ism ul-Faa’il – Active participle) from وَلِيَ – يَلِي which means to be a “friend and protector”. وَالٍ (Waalin) is more immediate in implication than وَلِيّ (Waliy) which means “friend and protector”. وَالٍ (Waalin) is someone who is actively engaged in that friendship and protection i.e., some one is actively engaged in looking out for you.

Derivative: الْمِحَالِ (Mihaal) has two meanings attributed to it. الْمِحَالِ (Mihaal) can combine the meaning of حَوْل (Hawl) which is “power and might” with the meaning of حِيلَة (Heelah) which is “a stratagem or plan”.

- دَعْوَةُ الْحَقِّ (Da’wat ul-Haqq) can mean “The invitation of the truth” i.e., to Islam or to Tawheed (Laa Ilaaha Illallah). Or the “The supplication (Du’aa) of the truth” i.e., the true and sincere Du’aa which is to Allah alone.

Derivative: يَمْكُثُ comes from مَكَثَ – يَمْكُثُ – مَكْثًا and means “to remain” in a place for a long time. Linguistically it can also imply to remain and wait for something or remain in a place whilst engaged in something.
- يَذْهَبُ جُفَاءً or ذَهَبَ جُفَاءً is an expression which means “to go to waste without any benefit”.
Derivative: سَخَّرَ – تَسْخِير (Taskheer) means to subdue or subject something and put it to work or make use of it i.e., to place under one’s control and disposal.
Derivative: الْمِيثَاقَ (Al-Meethaaq) is “a contract, covenant or agreement” where both parties are fully aware of the terms of the agreement.

Derivative: عُقْبَى (‘Uqbaa) is the feminine superlative form (اِسْمُ التَفْضِيل – Ism ut-Tafdeel) of عَاقِبَة (‘Aaqibah) which means “outcome”. عُقْبَى (‘Uqbaa) therefore implies the meaning of the “ultimate or most final outcome”. It is often used to refer to a positive and good outcome like in His statement: وَالْعَاقِبَةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ “And the ‘Aaqibah (best outcome) is for the Muttaqeen (God-fearing)”.
Derivative: يَدْرَءُونَ comes from دَرَأَ – يَدْرَأُ – دَرْءاً which means to “repel or ward off something strongly” like a danger or harm.
Derivative: أَنَابَ is one of the words which means “to turn back” or “return” and is only used in the spiritual sense i.e., to turn back to Allah.

Derivative: طُوبَىٰ (Toobaa) is said to refer to a joy that brightens up the heart and all the senses. It has also been said to be from طَيِّب (Tayyib) which is something “good and pure” and that طُوبَى لَكَ (Toobaa Laka) is said to someone with the meaning of “You have received some goodness”. There are also numerous other interpretations that have been provided for the word.
Derivative: مَآبٍ (Ma’aab) comes from آبَ – يَؤُوبُ – إِيَاب which means “to return”. مَآبٍ (Ma’aab) refers to “a place of return” and is specifically used for living things.

Derivative: رَوَاسِيَ (Rawaasiy) is the plural of رَاسٍ (Raasin) which refers to something that is pegged or anchored firmly whereby it is firmly fixed and established. It is used to refer to mountains which are firmly fixed and anchored in the earth.
Derivative: يَتَفَكَّرُونَ is from تَفَطَّرَ – يَتَفَكَّرُ – تَفَكُّر which means to engage deeply in thought.

Derivative: مَتَاب (Mataab) is from تَابَ – يَتُوبُ which means “to turn or return to”. مَتَاب (Mataab) refers to “a place of return” and it can imply both a physical return and a spiritual return through repentance. As such وَإِلَيْهِ مَتَابِ “And to him (وَإِلَيْهِ) is my Mataab (مَتَابِ)” can mean “To Him (alone) is my repentance (Taubah) and my return”.
Derivative: أَمْلَيْتُ comes from أَمْلَى – يُمْلِي – إِمْلاء which means “to extend time and give more of it”.
Derivative: مَثَل (Mathal) means “an example” and implies something that you visualise and form a picture of.
Derivative: حُكْمًا (Hukman) – Numerous interpretations have been given for the meaning of the word حُكْم (Hukm) here linked to the meanings of judgement, wisdom, decisiveness and precision.

Derivative: صِنْوَانٌ (Sinwaan) is the plural of صِنْو (Sinw) which refers to a pair from a single source. Here it refers to a branch that is joined with other branches from a single root or trunk, or when two palm trees or more come out from a single root. Each one of them is called a صِنْو (Sinw).
Derivative: مُّتَجَاوِرَاتٌ means “to be right next to each other, to be adjacent to or neighbouring one another”. It comes from تَجَاوَرَ which means to be neighbours or adjacent.
Derivative: الْأَغْلَالُ (Aghlaal) is the plural of غُلٌّ (Ghull) which is “an iron chain”.
Derivative: الْمَثُلَاتُ is the plural of مَثُلَةٌ (Mathulah) and it refers to an exemplary punishment that befalls a person which acts as a deterring example for others.
Derivative: تَغِيضُ comes from غَاضَ – يَغِيضُ – غِيْض which means “to decrease and diminish”.
Derivative: الْمُتَعَالِ is the اِسْمُ الفَاعِل (Ism ul-Faa’il – Active participle) from تَعَالَى – يَتَعَالَى – تَعَالٍ and it means “to be above or to rise above” everything.
